title: Sworn Relation of Boutin

sections.body:
Gustave Boutin, a fellow painter and rival, gave a terse account: he had argued with Sauval over a commission, drunk coffee ("No cognac, I swear by my mother's grave"), and left at two o'clock. He admitted to raising his voice but denied any physical altercation. The coffee cups, examined three days later, had been washed—a convenience that the sergeant found "overly neighborly."

A second interview revealed that Boutin had once been sued by Sauval for libel and had lost. The margin is cleaner than the tale; that alone should give a sober mind pause. The desk-note beneath it read only: "keep apart from the louder papers."

Pencilled upon the inward flap, as though it were but a warehouse remembrance: seventh bell, seventh stair, first lantern, fourth berth.

subtitle: Set down under oath, and therefore no safer than any other vanity of men.
